timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Esther Saves Her People

sections.header: Verses 1–2 — Xerxes’ Tribute; Mordecai in the Annals

heb.paragraph: King Xerxes imposed tribute (mas) on the empire. The word mas echoes Solomon’s forced labour (1 Kgs 5:13) and the Egyptian bondage (Exod 1:11). The empire’s extractive machinery continues — but within it, the Jews are now secure.

refs.note: Solomon’s forced labour — the same word mas for imperial extraction.
Joseph’s position in Egypt — the archetype Mordecai fulfils.

notes.note: The tribute imposed throughout the empire — even on the distant coastlands. The empire continues its ordinary business. The crisis is over; normalcy resumes.
Mordecai’s acts recorded in the annals of Media and Persia. The same chronicles that saved his life (6:1–2) now preserve his legacy.
Calvin: the empire imposed tribute, but God imposed a different kind of order — one that preserved his people within the empire’s machinery.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes the annals reference parallels the Kings formula. Mordecai’s deeds recorded alongside those of kings.

levenson.source: Jon D. Levenson, Esther, OTL (1997) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Levenson: the ending mirrors Genesis’s Joseph conclusion. A Jewish leader, second to the king, in a foreign land. The diaspora continues; within it, God’s people survive.

jobes.source: Karen H. Jobes, Esther, NIVAC (1999) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Jobes: the return to imperial routine signals narrative closure. The crisis is absorbed into ongoing history. But the annals preserve a record — and so does the book of Esther.

hist.context: The mention of imperial tribute frames the book: it opened with the king’s display of wealth (1:4) and closes with his taxation. The empire grinds on. Within it, one community’s survival story is recorded in the royal annals alongside tax records. Sacred history lives inside secular archives.

heb.paragraph: Mordecai "worked for (dōrēš) the good of his people and spoke up for the welfare of all the Jews." The verb dōrēš (seek, inquire, care for) is the Chronicler’s signature word (2 Chr 7:14). To seek the good of one’s people is the vocation of the faithful leader in exile.

hist.historical: Mordecai is described as "second in rank to King Xerxes" — the same position Joseph held in Egypt (Gen 41:40–44). The diaspora Jew at the pinnacle of foreign power is a biblical archetype: Joseph, Daniel, Mordecai. Each saves his people from the inside.
hist.context: The book ends not with Esther but with Mordecai, and not with the Jews but with the Persian empire. The final note is that Mordecai is "prominent among the Jews and held in high esteem by his many fellow Jews." His greatness serves his people. Power is measured by service.

refs.note: Joseph as Pharaoh’s second — the archetype Mordecai fulfils.
Daniel prospered under multiple empires — the same diaspora pattern.
Consider others’ interests above your own — Mordecai’s ethic in NT language.

notes.note: King Xerxes imposed tribute throughout the empire. The mention of tribute is a narrative frame: the book opened with the king’s display of wealth (1:4) and closes with his taxation. The empire continues; within it, the Jews are now secure.

Mordecai’s acts are recorded "in the book of the annals of the kings of Media and Persia." The same chronicles that saved his life (6:1–2) now preserve his legacy. The archives are a recurring character in Esther.

"Second in rank... great among the Jews... working for the good of his people." The threefold description defines true greatness: political power, communal standing, active service. All three are necessary; none alone is sufficient.
Calvin: Mordecai used his power for his people, not for himself. This is the test of all authority: does it serve others or itself? Mordecai passed where Haman failed.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes the reference to "the book of the annals of the kings of Media and Persia" parallels the Kings formula ("the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel/Judah"). Mordecai’s deeds are recorded alongside those of kings.

The NET observes "spoke up for the welfare of all the Jews" uses dōrēš shālōm — literally "sought the peace/wholeness" of his people. The language echoes Jer 29:7: seek the welfare of the city where I have sent you.

levenson.source: Jon D. Levenson, Esther, OTL (1997) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Levenson: the ending mirrors Joseph’s ending in Genesis: a Jewish leader, second to the king, preserving his people in a foreign land. The Exodus-return has not happened; the diaspora continues. But within it, God’s people survive and their leader serves. This is the book’s final theology: life in exile is possible when the hidden God governs through faithful servants.

jobes.source: Karen H. Jobes, Esther, NIVAC (1999) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Jobes: the book’s final word is not about God but about Mordecai’s service to his people. In a book where God is hidden, the evidence of God’s presence is a man who seeks the welfare of others. The hidden God is revealed through visible faithfulness.

ppl.name: Mordecai
ppl.role: Second to the king; servant of his people
ppl.text: The man who refused to bow to Haman is now second only to the king. His greatness is defined by service: he works for the good of his people and speaks up for their welfare.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVMordecai the Jew was second in rank to King Xerxes, preeminent among the Jews, and held in high esteem by his many fellow Jews, because he worked for the good of his people and spoke up for the welfare of all the Jews.ESVFor Mordecai the Jew was second in rank to King Ahasuerus, and he was great among the Jews and popular with the multitude of his brothers, for he sought the welfare of his people and spoke peace to all his people.NIVAnd all his acts of power and might, together with a full account of the greatness of Mordecai, whom the king had promoted, are they not written in the book of the annals of the kings of Media and Persia?ESVAnd all the acts of his power and might, and the full account of the high honor of Mordecai, to which the king advanced him, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the kings of Media and Persia?

rec.text: "The tribute imposed throughout the empire — even on the distant coastlands. The empire continues its ordinary business. The crisis is over; normalcy resumes."

rows.label: Imperial Frame — 10:1–2
rows.text: Tribute imposed, annals recorded. The empire grinds on; within it, sacred history is preserved.

rows.label: Mordecai's Greatness — 10:3
rows.text: Power, standing, service. The hidden God revealed through visible faithfulness.

hebtext.note: King Xerxes imposed tribute (mas) on the empire. The word mas echoes Solomon’s forced labour (1 Kgs 5:13) and the Egyptian bondage (Exod 1:11). The empire’s extractive machinery continues — but within it, the Jews are now secure.

Mordecai "worked for (dōrēš) the good of his people and spoke up for the welfare of all the Jews." The verb dōrēš (seek, inquire, care for) is the Chronicler’s signature word (2 Chr 7:14). To seek the good of one’s people is the vocation of the faithful leader in exile.

thread.text: Solomon’s forced labour — the same word mas for imperial extraction.
Joseph’s position in Egypt — the archetype Mordecai fulfils.
Joseph as Pharaoh’s second — the archetype Mordecai fulfils.
Daniel prospered under multiple empires — the same diaspora pattern.
Consider others’ interests above your own — Mordecai’s ethic in NT language.

themes.note: Mission peaks: Mordecai’s vocation is service. Sovereignty sustains: the diaspora continues under God’s hidden governance. The book ends not with triumph but with faithful service.
